text: An eighty-year-old female patient searched for medical assistance complaining of a metocasia, anaprolapse and progressive constipation. She also referred to severe weight loss. Under physical examination, a voluminous irreducible anaprolapse was noted. Rectal digital examination has shown a movable and soft voluminous lesion extending from the lower to the medium rectum. Although the patient had a poor performance status, she had no severe comorbidities. The patient had undergone a complete colonoscopy two years earlier, showing two synchronous lesions, one in rectum with similar characteristics and a more advanced lesion in mid sigmoid, although allowing completion of the examination at that occasion. A new colonoscopy was performed, but it was not possible to trespass the proximal lesion.

Her upstaging with CT scans and endorectal ultrasound of the distal lesion has shown a senosal lesion in mid sigmoid and an UT-INO lesion in mid and lower rectum. Considering her age and poor performance status, we decided to perform a trans-anal minimally invasive surgery using CILS-PORT to resect the rectal lesion and at the same procedure, a laparoscopic anterior rectosigmoidectomy with colorectal end-to-end anastomosis.

The surgeon inserts a lubricated disposable SILS port for single-port laparoscopy, creating a pneumo-rectum to provide space for dissection. The dissection begins four centimeters from the anal verge, progressing cephalad with a combination of sharp and blunt techniques. The limited space initially complicates the procedure, but as dissection continues, more space becomes available, allowing the lesion to be fully mobilized and released while maintaining adequate margins.

After excising the rectal tumor, the surgeon leaves the extraperitoneal defect open following hemostasis and wound revision. The SILS port is removed, and the tumor is collected. The patient is repositioned in a modified Light-Davis position for the laparoscopic abdominal approach. The surgeon mobilizes the left colon and splenic flexure from medial to lateral. The inferior mesenteric vein is identified, isolated, and ligated using ligature blunt tip forceps, which are also used to seal the inferior mesenteric artery. This dissection creates a window to access Toad's fascia, revealing the left ureter and gonadal vessels.

The surgeon opens the pre-sacral plane and identifies the tattooed segment of the colon. The upper rectum is skeletonized, and the recto-sigma junction is stapled using an endo-GIA stapler. The left lateral gutter is fully mobilized. A peri-umbilical incision of approximately four centimeters is made for specimen retrieval and the introduction of the circular stapler anvil. The procedure concludes with incisional closure and the performance of an end-to-end anastomosis.

The anastomosis and the rectal defect were endoscopically controlled for hemostasis and integrity at the end of the procedure. This patient had an uneventful recovery and was discharged home at day six. Pathology revealed a PT-III and I sigmoidal and PT-II specimens, in rectum in this case, both with free distal, proximal and circumferential margins. Up to this moment, the patient is free of disease and in follow-up at our colorectal unit and by the clinical oncology team.
